literal: fashioning, measuring-out, world-forming power; often connected with √mā 'to measure, to form'
invariant_definition: *Māyā* has no single pan-Indian meaning. The thinnest common core is this: it names a power or condition through which manifest plurality is produced, sustained, or experienced. Beyond that, the term becomes school-specific. In Vedic and purāṇic usage it can mean divine skill, wondrous power, or formative capacity. In Advaita it becomes the cosmic register of *avidyā*, the principle by which the non-dual appears as manifold without undergoing real modification. In realist Vedāntas it names a real divine power, often identified with *prakṛti* or with the Lord's external energy. In Trika it is not illusion at all but a real self-contractive phase of Śiva's *svātantrya*, linked to the *kañcuka* analysis and the production of differentiated subjects and objects. The term therefore sits amid disputes over causality, *pariṇāma* versus *vivarta*, the status of the world, and the relation between God and manifestation. In practice *māyā* explains embodiment, limitation, and cosmic display, but what sort of explanation this is depends entirely on the metaphysics of the school using it.

per_school.definition: *Māyā* is the beginningless sleep of the *jīva* and the sole register in which duality appears, so that *prapañca* is *māyāmātra* while *advaita* alone is *paramārtha* [[MK 1.16-18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakaraṇa.16-18)]. The unborn is differentiated only *māyayā*, never *tattvataḥ*; real difference would compromise its *ajatva* and *amṛtatva* [[MK 3.19-21](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.19-21)]. Post-Śaṅkara Advaita identifies this *māyā* with *avidyā*: neither *sat* nor *asat*, but *anirvacanīyā*, operative in *vyavahāra* and located in the *jīvas* [[BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)]. In Vivaraṇa idiom, causal Brahman is Brahman qualified by *anirvacanīya-māyā*, and the same doctrine can be restated as *māyā* being *sadasadbhyām anirvacanīyā* [[PV p. 212](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahmaṇo lakṣaṇopalakṣaṇe), [IṢ 1.1](cite://vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Prapañcasyānirvacanīyatvam)].

citations.locus_short: BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)

per_school.definition: The on-disk Viśiṣṭādvaitin dossier handles *māyā* chiefly by rejecting the thesis that Brahman itself, ignorant and overrun by alien *upādhis*, becomes the saṃsārin; that teaching is condemned as *śruti-nyāyāpeta* [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §2](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§2)]. Positively, Bhagavān Nārāyaṇa is the *antaryāmin* of the entire *cid-acid-prapañca*, taught through expressions such as "his power," "his body," and "his manifestation"; the world-order is therefore a real dependence-structure, not a merely apparent projection [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. Beginningless ignorance belongs to the embodied *jīva*'s karmic entanglement and is remedied through knowledge of the *dehātirikta ātman* and upāsanā of the indwelling Lord, not by cancelling the reality of the cosmos [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)].

When the term is accepted positively, *māyā* is Hari's own power: *ātma-māyā* is glossed as *svarūpabhūtecchā*, a will intrinsic to the Lord rather than an ontic screen over reality [[Bhāg. 1.1.18](cite://madhva/bhagavata-tatparya-nirnaya/1.1.18), [Bhāg. 1.1.18 — gloss](cite://madhva/bhagavata-tatparya-nirnaya/1.1.18 — gloss)]. Polemically, no *ajñāna* can coherently function as an *āvaraṇa*, since there is no admissible unestablished substrate for it to cover [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, "Nothing can serve as a covering ignorance"](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/can serve as a covering ignorance)]. For that reason the entire *māyāvāda* is rejected as incoherent, and the world's difference is not downgraded to a merely apparent status [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, "Therefore the whole doctrine is set aside"](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/the whole doctrine is set aside), [Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, concluding verse](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/verse of refutation)].
primary_loci: Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, 'Nothing can serve as a covering ignorance'
Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, 'Therefore the whole doctrine is set aside'

cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/the whole doctrine is set aside

per_school.definition: The *abheda-śāstras* are retained only after the rival *pariccheda* and *pratibimba* readings are subordinated to Bhagavān's *svābhāvika acintya-śakti* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42), [Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Jīvas are *cid-rūpa* yet stand to Bhagavān as ray-atoms to the sun, so *bheda* and *avyatireka* are both preserved without collapsing into *svarūpābheda* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Within that settlement, Bhagavān is *māyāśraya* and the bound *jīva* is *māyā-mohita*; precisely this difference makes *bhagavad-bhajana* the school's *abhidheya* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)].

Nimbārka describes the *jīva* as bearing a form associated with beginningless *māyā*; bondage, devotion, and release are real conditions of that dependent self and are known only by Bhagavān's grace [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]. On the side of *acit*, the insentient order is explicitly denoted by terms such as *māyā* and *pradhāna* [[DaŚ 3](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/3)]. *Māyā* therefore names a real, dependent non-conscious principle within the *bhedābheda* order, not an *anirvacanīya* cancellation of the world's reality [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2), [DaŚ 3](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/3)].

Śuddhādvaita refuses to make *nāma-rūpa* into an illusory screen: Brahman itself is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa*, while name and form are *kārya-dharmas* [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. The world is a real *pariṇāma* of Brahman, though Brahman remains *avikṛta* in the alaukika manner illustrated by gold and its products [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. *Avidyā-nivṛtti* prepares the *jīva* for Puruṣottama, but attainment is completed through *bhakti* and *varaṇa* rather than by sublating a *mithyā-jagat* [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

Vijñānabhikṣu explicitly glosses *māyā* as *prakṛti* and *māyin* as Maheśvara, so the term denotes the cosmological non-conscious principle rather than an epistemic indeterminable [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)]. This is the late synthetic Sāṃkhya-Yoga-Vedānta line preserved on disk: *māyā* is relocated into the ontology of *prakṛti*-governed manifestation under the Lord [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)].

In the on-disk Yogācāra dossier, *māyā* is not the load-bearing cosmological term; misleading objecthood is analysed through *asadarthāvabhāsa* and *vijñaptimātratā* [[Vvs 1](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/1)]. Dream, preta-vision, and hell-experience show that *deśa-kāla-niyama*, *saṃtānāniyama*, and even efficacy can obtain although the appearing object is unreal [[Vvs 3](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Preta Replies), [Vvs 4](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Hell)]. What ordinary thought takes as object and subject is *parikalpita* alone, so the explanatory work later polemics often assign to *māyā* is here redistributed to *vikalpa*, *bīja*, and *vijñāna-pariṇāma* [[Triṃśikā 17](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/17), [Triṃśikā 20](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/20), [Vvs 10](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/10 and Entry into *Dharma-Nairātmya*)].

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'illusion.' That is seriously misleading. Most schools using *māyā* do not mean subjective illusion at all, and even Advaita does not mean simple unreality or *asat*. The word can import very different metaphysics: cosmic ignorance, real divine power, material nature, or Śiva's self-contraction. English should therefore signal the school-specific use instead of flattening everything into 'illusion.'
